speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two. You were talking about escaping in the cart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you can--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's interesting because I-- I claimed to-- to be privileged to have set up some sort of a world record. People love-- like to set world records. By that, I mean when we were smuggled out and taken to the partisans, the guards that smugglers us when my father proved that he was a doctor were given two bottles of vodka for each one of us. When they got into a cottage in the forest and took us to the-- to the German officer that put us into the ghetto, they were given one bottle of vodka for each one of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my life actually is worth three bottles of vodka. And there's very few people that go both ways because you either go one way or the other way. To get three bottles of vodka, you've got to be somebody or other or you're lucky because you're talking to somebody. My life was worth three bottles of vodka. OK. May I ask you, how was it for you being hidden all the time like in this cart that you were taken from the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That didn't last long. That was at night, only went for a few hours because we got into the forest [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: As a little boy, what was the feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, once we got out of the-- once we got out of the camp, we were able to breathe. The cart stopped. There were two guards who were both sitting in front, but we couldn't expose ourselves in case the Germans would pass by or something. And they were going up and down in trucks and trains and so on. Everything was going and we were just going in a cart with clothing and shoes they were taking to whatever they were going to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think that was happening? Did you know what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. I knew we're going-- we're going to the forest and we're going to be OK. So-- so that was good. You know, I was quite happy with that arrangement. Very hungry at-- at this stage. And that was bad-- bad news that there wasn't much to eat. And once we got through the forest, interrogation and then my mother and I were straightaway taken to the village. We're still doing the night before daylight, all this happened through the night. My father stayed in the forest with the partisans. My mother and I were taken to the village. While we were going in the same cart, it went back to the village, the [INAUDIBLE] cart, I was changed-- it was all arranged, everything was-- I was changing to a girl's clothing. And now I become a girl and my name was Marisha. It was easy to remember because it was [? Rishik, ?] it's Polish. Richard is [? Rishik, ?] Marisha is the feminine. So I become [? Marisha. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it for you? You were always told that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Being a girl? Oh, it was good fun. It was good fun. I had to-- I had a problem-- I had a problem being a girl. It wasn't so easy. But then I guess someone said I must-- I probably wasn't stupid because I was acting stupid. You see, I was meant to be a retarded girl because I couldn't speak like a girls- like girls speak. Feminine in Polish is different to masculine. We don't speak like in English. It's different, so I couldn't speak in a feminine way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was retarded. I wasn't supposed to say too much and I was meant to be a bit awkward. So I wouldn't have to go to school because I was now eight years old and if I went to school, I'd be exposed. And the reason why they made me a girl is because as a boy it didn't work too well and they don't look if a girl is circumcised. So I was much safer. I had really long hair. I hadn't had a haircut for two and a half years, so-- so my hair grew. It wasn't fashionable in those days, but then who cared about the fashion? So I looked up-- they made me look like a girl, yes. They made me look like a retard girl. I looked different. So everyone teased me in the village because the next day and so on, my mother was supposed to be a sister of one of the-- or relative of one of the villagers and I was her retarded child and my father had perished. He was an officer in the Polish Army, Royal Polish, and he perished.

speaker: interviewee
text: And well, but we were with sympathizers of the partisans so we were OK and nobody knew except the people-- the family that we were with, the farm that I was a Jewish boy. So but they were in secretive because their-- their son was also [INAUDIBLE] my father and so on. And that masquerading lasted for about three or four months, which wasn't all that bad. Kids used to-- used to tease me, but I developed a defensive mechanism. I've been able to-- I had the ability to see something or other. I mean, I was obviously developing into-- into a survivor already. And-- and I just-- I-- I developed a very good way of speaking. I used to spit on the children. [SPITS] And I used to spit two, three meters away. I become a very good spitter. Well, not being able to go to school so I couldn't be-- I couldn't get any first class honors yet in those days, so I developed my skills in spitting. I used to spit and scream at the kids. And they left me alone because they didn't like to be spat on. They thought I was wild and-- and dangerous. So I used to go to church, but not to school. And I didn't have to [INAUDIBLE] because I was retarded and I didn't have to go to school because I was retarded. And that went all right for three or four months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother do during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was helping on the farm and cooking and doing things and so on and so forth. And she was getting news and telling us that my father's OK. During the night, partisans used to come pick up food for them and because they were-- the were the families who were sympathetic to the partisans. And life become quite normal. I mean, I wasn't normal, but life was normal. So that's that. After a while, it become more dangerous for me to stay. There were some rumors about-- about them coming around and looking for Jews and so on and so on. So I went back to the forest, and this time in-- back into boys' clothing, a little bit bigger than me. Just as well because the war lasted for much longer than we thought. So I wore the same trousers for another couple of years in the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I rejoined the partisans and then I spent the last 18 months of the war with the partisans, reunited with my father. Did anybody take you there, or how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's [INAUDIBLE], partisans who came to get food. In the middle of the night they always came and took food and they brought me to the forest together with them. And I said goodbye to mom. I spent 19 months, two winters, in the forest [INAUDIBLE] on trees. And temperatures used to get down to minus 30 or so. It gets pretty cold in the Russian forest-- Ukrainian forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mom stay behind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She stayed in the village. Yes, she stayed in the village and survived. And I rejoined-- I wasn't with my father, I was with the partisans. And-- and they dressed me like-- they gave me a cap and-- and I was introduced to the leader, whose name was Jurek. I still remember him. He had a tattered Polish uniform. He was an officer in the Polish Army who escaped. He was in charge. Jurek was his name. And they called him Captain Jurek. They used to joke, they used to call him General Jurek. They made him a-- made-- but he was a nice man. And-- and I was hanging around a bit like a bad smell, not knowing what to do with the partisans and they didn't know what to do with me, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they used to smoke and they used to give me funny cigarettes. I didn't know and I found out afterwards they used to put leaves in mine, just leaves and paper. So I would smoke like them but I wouldn't smoke tobacco I was just smoking dried leaves. Maybe that's better for my health. Maybe we should all be smoking leaves. So there you are, I started at a young age smoking weed. It wasn't pot, it was just leaves.

speaker: interviewee
text: But so, I did like them and-- and they would water down my vodka because I always have [INAUDIBLE], you know, you get in before you go-- before you go to an expedition that could be your last one and many of them were. You have a few drinks, it doesn't hurt as much to get killed. And-- but mine was all watered down. Man, it tasted terrible. [INAUDIBLE]. But I had to drink. Big partisan! [INAUDIBLE] are big boy, so I had to become a fully fledged partisan [INAUDIBLE]. They gave me a job. I had a good job. They decided after a little while to take me on a-- on a sortie because we were-- we were guarding the hospitals and so. It was different groups of partisans all scattered all over the forest. And so I became the feather boy. He had a sense of humor, that leader, and he gave me a father and I became known as the feather boy. And what we used to do is they used to ambush German soldiers, German patrols, only in small quantities. And there was no shooting, shooting on in self-defense but very rarely that it happened. Towards the end of the war, yes, but not-- they-- they-- there-- although they carried guns, there-- there are ways to ambush them, bayonet and hold them up and cut the neck, just slice it from-- from ear to ear. And it takes only two or three seconds to die or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they dropped them dead, but sometimes they didn't do such a good job. And I was the feather boy, so they gave me a feather and when the soldiers dropped, I used to put the feather under the nose and count up to 100. They asked me if I could count and I could, but no longer on my hand. Now I could do it loudly. So I would count up to 100 and if the feather didn't move, I went to the next one-- one or two, whatever they were numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: If the feather moved, I called them over and they gave it another go. They gave him another bayonet just to finish off. Occasionally [INAUDIBLE] I learned my first year words in German during that time because there were some that were injured but they sort of survived a little bit longer and they had enough strength to still pull out photographs and they used to point out to their children-- kinder, frau; kinder, frau. They were showing me. They were the first German words I learned, you know, trying to-- to sort of save their life or whatever. But they explained to me that and I thought it was a bit cruel to kill a man that's got children and wives, but we knew where we were and what business we were because they took no prisoners and we took no prisoners. You see, if we get caught, we get killed. So the-- the captain explained that ti's better that they die than we die. For every one of them that dies, one of us survives. So the game was good. Then we took off the boots. I used to carry the boots around my neck. We took off all the leather-- you know, belts and things like that. If the clothing was good, we took that-- caps, this, that, and so on. We carried all the loot back with us and that was good fun. I had a job. I was-- I felt like being part of the machinery. This-- this was called self-defense. I mean, it was-- it was [INAUDIBLE] murder, but better them than us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you cope as an eight-year-old with seeing people being killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was normal. I've seen so many of us dying in the-- in the-- in the ghettos and so on, it was nice to see some of them dying. I think it was wonderful. I thought it was-- I was doing a good job. I was a good feather boy. I was the best there was. I was the best there was. So-- winters were cold, two of them. In the first, I grew out of my shoes so I had rags. That was bad to move all night because we're hidden in shallow trees, sometimes underground. In summer, it was good fun because I was taught to breathe like a fish. So we'd lie in the water if there was any Germans or anybody around and the guards, so we'd get under the water and you would just leave you mouth up under a leaf. You get a leaf, you hold the leaf and nothing-- just breath like a fish, quietly, quietly. Just breathe like a fish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a river or a creek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [INAUDIBLE]. The Ukrainian forests, you get lots of water, you know, in our boots-- but I'd get into not too deep so I wouldn't drown, of course. I couldn't swim then. But they were just-- under water there's lots of this, lots of water. And we were pretty excited because they would get bogged. You see, the Germans didn't want to get in the water. They couldn't go by car or motorcycle or anything. So if they went there, that's when they got their-- their-- their throat cut. If they got lost, this is the way they got killed in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did a bit of that. And-- but a lot of our partisans got caught, too. I mean, it went both ways. And people disappeared and they got killed, so that's that. But that went on like that for a couple of winters. Two-- two events that stood out most of all, except my usual work, was there was at one stage a very, very heavy bombing. And it was towards the end of the war, as it happened, and the retreating German soldiers going to the forest and the Russians were bombing the hell out of them and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and the Germans were bombing, the Russians were bombing. That was really what they were retreating from, the Russian front. And it was a terrible night one night. I was sitting on a tree. In the morning, I came down, there was lots of wounded and killed partisans. And everybody was very sad, very sad because many of their friends were just blown up. I was in a lucky tree, I had a lucky run. And when I came down from the tree, I found a leg. It was winter-- winter of '45, the last winter we spent in the forest. And I found a leg which was a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Winter. Which was the last winter I spent? No, 1944. Yes, yes, because [INAUDIBLE] Ukrainian. Sorry. Winter of '44, yes. I'm dreaming, pardon me. And I found a leg which was my size, still had a boot. I don't know who it belonged to because the rest was blown up. But I got that leg and I was trying to tell the officer that I'm taking that leg to my father, who was about 300 meters away in the hospital. And-- and he was very upset. And I couldn't understand why. So [INAUDIBLE]. He turned around. So I don't know why he was upset, but he was upset because evidently, the leg belonged to someone that he knew. But the rest was not good, the leg was good. So I took the leg with me, took it to my father. It took me half a day, about maybe three or four hours, to cover 200 or 300 meters because the snow was very soft and I to sink in and the leg used to sink in where the [INAUDIBLE] the leg was toward me. And we would get up and down and I would crawl up the leg and push the leg up and the leg would go down and I would go down. And we kept on going and going, going and it just lasted forever. It was fantastic. I thought it was a good game. And I was doing something very useful, of course, taking a good leg to my father who was fixing people. So I mean, it's one of the best ones there was. I had a good look at it. I-- I wasn't stupid, don't worry about that. I-- before I found, I took the best leg. You know, I wouldn't have taken anything-- anything-- anything but the best. So I took that good leg to my father. I had to wait for a while, but he came in and the first time I've seen him actually in-- all covered in blood, his white overalls. He was wearing lots of blood and he had blood-- they were operating because some of the wounded were already taken, the ones that were alive. You see, they got there before me. I mean, it took me six hours. They would all die by then. I mean, they were already in hospital being stitched up, the ones that were still alive. I got a dead fellow's leg. And so he took that leg. He had a look at the leg and he put it against the post. And I was trying to explain to him it was the best there was. And he tapped me on my head and he was saying something like, gee, that's terrible. Well, there's something-- I can't remember exactly what he was saying. But anyway, he started crying. And I-- I was surprised. I hadn't seen anyone crying for-- for a long time. I mean, you know, partisans don't cry, big boys don't cry. Crying is always [INAUDIBLE] allowed to cry, I told myself. I figured [INAUDIBLE] a partisan, not like us. I mean, we partisans don't cry. We-- we live or we die. We kill or we die, but we don't cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was crying and crying. And anyhow, he said he's got to go back to-- to work because he's in the middle of-- of looking after sick people, trying to make them not sick people. Sick people. People with legs and arms missing were sick people. That's why I brought the leg and he wasn't fooling me. And they weren't just sick people. So I said, OK. And I walked away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- no, my father walked away and then I walked away and he waved to me. And then I came back. I was all upset because e went away and left the leg standing against the pole. I came back and I told my father, look, it took me three hours to bring that leg. Oh, and he felt sorry. he took the leg and he was still crying. And I went away. [EXHALES] That's the last time I saw my father alive. OK. I went back--

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time that you were partisan, did you meet your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few times, yes. But not that often because he was in hospital. But that's the last time. And I went back to-- to them, to the partisans, and we spent the next few months mainly in hiding because it was very dangerous to-- to-- there were lots of Germans coming there to be around. So we didn't do much killing, we were just hiding most of the time. But in April, that went shortly after that episode, about a month or so later, I was given what has become-- what has been my greatest present ever in my life, and I was given a bread, a whole bread. It's called the-- one of those peasant bread. It's black and it's hard and it's-- and it's heavy and it's got lots of sawdust, very little wheat. But anyhow, it was good. Now, that bread was mine. So I put the bread inside my shirt and I scratched on it right up till the Russians came over, which was from April till about August. For about three or four months I was scratching on that bread. It was my bread, my bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anything else to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that stage, we were looking-- oh, yes. We eat whatever we can. I mean, in the forest it was like that. I mean, the law was basically if it's-- if it's small and you can catch it, you eat it and keep quiet about it. If it's big, you don't catch it, you keep away from it because it might eat you. So you keep away from bears and things like that. I'm not stupid enough to grab them or wolves or things. But if it's small-- rabbits, rice, and mice, rats, any of those things you can eat. You grab the meat and quietly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you cook it? Did you have a chance to cook it?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't cook anything because if you cook, you show the smoke comes up. There's no cooking, we eat raw. When they bring-- bring from the village, they bring that were cooked. The peasant families and so on used to cook meat for us or whatever soup they would carry in parcels. But we didn't cook. You cannot cook in the forest. You eat what you eat. What you catch, you eat. I mean, you supplement yourself, you know. Especially in summer and spring, you get lots of things that are growing-- the flowers and grass and things like that. Even when the bark is the only thing that you can eat if you're hungry, besides the food that you get. But it's not all that exciting. I-- I-- I never got to like bark. I like the greenery or whatever I catch. It was OK. Butterflies, you know, flies, [INAUDIBLE] they were OK, an occasional rat or mice. But-- but the others, I wasn't good enough to catch rabbits. They were too quick for me. The others used to set traps and used to catch rabbits and slice them up, dry them up, or take them to the village to cook. I don't know what they did with them. Anyhow, we'd get him back. But bark was never any good. I never got to like the bark. I used to-- I had my handkerchiefs. I had lots of things-- things that-- I ate threads. I used to get very hungry, I used to eat-- chew away at whatever I was wearing and then get something new when the-- when the occasion came. But bark was never any good, no. I never got to like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other people around your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I was the only one and they only kept me because I was-- I was the doctor's son. I mean, they didn't need me. I thought I was a useful part of the killing machinery or the resistance force and I called myself a partisan-- big deal-- because I was the feather boy. But I mean, you know, I realize now in reality that I was really a nuisance not-- and-- and a bad smell. I mean, they needed me like a whole in the head. But nevertheless, I thought I was really doing-- you know, I was part of the business. But-- so that was-- that was-- at least they made me feel useful. Then the Russians came and some of the partisans joined the Russians Army, some of them went back to the village and become civilians. I went back with them, I was reunited with my mother. For the first time I've seen her in nearly two years, over 18 months. She had one look at me and she said, you've got a dirty mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: I still remember that today. She said, where is your hankie? I said, I ate the hankie. What? You ate the hankie? She started saying, [INAUDIBLE]. You haven't got a hankie? I said, oh, come on. I mean, I spent two years in partisans, and she's worried about a dirty mouth. Till that time, I didn't know what happened to my father because they kept on saying that he was taken prisoner. So I kept telling Mum. Mum knew that he had been exterminated. But-- but I didn't, see.

speaker: interviewee
text: The hospital was overrun, Germans that were retreating when all-- they executed all the wounded. They took the doctors with them for their own use and executed them somewhere further down the line. But I didn't know so I kept on looking for him. I kept on looking for him right up until about 1946, '47. For about two or three years I kept on writing letters. I wrote letters to Stalin, I wrote letters to Churchill. They didn't answer me. I don't know why. They didn't think I was important, I suppose, and so-- they played a game with me. People played a game with me. People through I was stupid, which I wasn't. But still, they said, why don't you write to Churchill, why don't you write to Stalin to tell me where my father was.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, different people. In an orphanage-- when I was in the displaced people's camp and orphanage. So that came to an end. We reunited with my mother. We come back to our village-- a town, not village, a town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was summer, the weather was warm. That's all I remember. No, I can't remember the day. I'd be lying to you. I mean, I can look up the date and I know where it was, but I can't remember exactly. But it was towards the end of '44 and it was before-- well, not end but about August, September '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We would have gone in before that because we were liberated. In '45 was already that-- now I'm getting confused. I think it is '45. Yes, because it wasn't my ninth birthday that I was given the bread. Must have been-- I can't remember exactly. I don't want to-- to speculate. But I remember going back to the village before the rest of Poland was liberated because we're a bit higher up, before Warsaw was liberated so it must have been-- when was Warsaw liberated? Do you know, by any chance? It would have been, what, towards the end of '45, 1945, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the war ended May '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now, that was end of '44. That was still-- we already finished-- we were in Poland, don't forget. So it was end of summer, autumn '44, before winter, when I went back to the village-- definitely. Because in '45 I was already in the displaced people's camp in Germany, the end of '45. That was the next year. And we went back to the village and there come the nasty bit. You know, this-- the guy wasn't all that good through the war, but things got worse for me. I started running out of luck. When we got up to the village, we went to visit some of my mother's friends who were still in rags. And she had friends, has it happened three Polish friends, wives of doctors that-- that my father was in charge of the hospital at the time and they were his supposedly good friends and the wives were supposed our good friends. We found out what they were made of. And when we got back there, we come to the first one-- and to first friend-- and they didn't want to know us. She pointed to the back, gave us a little bit of food, says, get away, something about the Jews are all gone, they couldn't believe us. And a similar thing happened to the second one. And my mother wasn't convinced yet. She went to her third friend, and believe it or not, the third friend was the family of my best friend, Helenka. And this was a-- a very sad moment, really. When she opened the door, Helenka recognized me. It's been five years. She was a nice-looking girl, very-- very attractive little 11-year-old girl and [INAUDIBLE]. She called out my name and her mother grabbed her and she fell over. She said, that's not Richard. All the Jews are dead. And she slammed the door. Whew. I was upset. That was bad news. that was bad news. [DRINKS] That's the memory I have of Poland. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After the war, anti-Semitism was even worse than it was before and during. And they were doctors' wives, but they had no use for-- for us anymore. My father was no longer in charge of the hospital, he was dead. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After that, we were told it was dangerous to be in-- in Radom. We went to Lowicz, where there was a group-- more Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you that it was dangerous?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mayor. My mother went, tried to recognize, say my father died and so on. They said, look, go away. My father died fighting for the country. Sad. OK. We went to-- to Lowicz, and there we met more Jewish people. My uncle and auntie came from Russia, they came to Lowicz. They organized to smuggle-- to smuggle out of-- from Poland, to get out because it's too dangerous. There were pogroms and so on. It was no good to stay there. We went to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --we stayed about maybe a few months in Poland, actually. Because I-- no, no, no. Longer than that. I'm-- I'm-- I'm beginning-- I'm getting confused. I spent three months in a sanatorium in Poland because I had contracted TB during the war. Some-- the food wasn't all that nutritious and fresh air didn't do me much good. And so I spent three or four months in a sanatorium in [POLISH] after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH], [POLISH], which was close to-- I also remember close to-- in the mountains, but not far from Warsaw. And because we went through Warsaw and Warsaw was completely flattened, there was nothing left-- totally different to the Warsaw we just saw before the war, totally different. It was completely flattened. But we went through on train and went to [POLISH] And I spent three and a half to four months in [POLISH] I learned to play chess in [POLISH] because that's all I could do. I was recovering. I ate well. ate well. I set some records, once again. I-- I ate 20 slices of bread, something or other, and butter on my first sitting. I had ears that stuck out. I've still get some photographs of myself. My ears were bigger than the rest of my head.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive medication also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. I was in a sanatorium. Yes. sure. Sure. I was well-treated and so on and so forth. I was given most of the things-- I had my first chocolate in about five years. And after that, we went-- we went to-- we tried to leave Poland because that was really-- now we're looking already at-- towards '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you where was your mother while you were in the sanatorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was in Lowicz together with her best friends. They were reunited with her-- with my father's best friend, Dr. Kasdan and his wife, who now live in Israel, very elderly. And they told me lots of things about my father which I didn't know, even a few years ago when I was a Israel. And not all that good, some of them, but he was a bit of a devil. I think I must have taken it from him, taken some shortcuts. Anyhow, that's that-- when he was young, of course. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would it be OK for you to say--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would it be OK for you to say a few things that they told you about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was a devil. Oh, and they said that he was-- they used to tell me because he knew him-- he went to school with him on so on-- that he was a bit of a womanizer. And in France, he-- he lost his ways a few times. He spent eight years studying in-- medicine in France [INAUDIBLE] and he sort of did a few-- did a few naughty things that everybody does but you don't think your father does. He's god. Your father wouldn't do that. He jumped out of windows and things like that they told me when husbands get home. [INAUDIBLE] also the things [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't tell you about the time during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They weren't together, no. They were in Russia. [INAUDIBLE], they all come from Russia, these people. Very few survived in Poland and so this was sort of-- and we-- we made arrangements to go to-- to run away from Poland. So we went to Czechoslovakia, we got caught; we finished up in jail for a few days; we were bought out by Joint, which is an American Jewish foundation that bought us out; and we finished up going to Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've finished another tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.
